title: Red Hat EX188V4K — Containers with Podman
description: Master container technology with Podman from environment setup through certification. Covers Podman fundamentals, Containerfiles, pods, registries, networking, storage, and troubleshooting — exactly what Red Hat tests on EX188.

modules.title: Module 1 — Environment Setup (free)
modules.summary: Get a Red Hat-compatible environment running on your machine without spending a rupee.

steps.title: Why this exam, what you'll build
steps.summary: Overview of EX188V4K: what Red Hat tests, hands-on format, why Podman replaces Docker in enterprise.
steps.content_md:
## Welcome

EX188V4K is Red Hat's **Containers with Podman** specialist exam. It's a **performance-based** exam — you'll be given real tasks on a real Red Hat system and graded on whether they work, not on multiple-choice answers.

**What you'll learn in this path:**

- Run a free RHEL-compatible environment (Fedora) at zero cost
- Podman fundamentals — the rootless successor to Docker
- Build images with `Containerfile`
- Multi-container apps using **pods** (Podman's killer feature)
- Networking, storage, registries, security, troubleshooting
- Quadlets for systemd-managed containers

Each module ends with a quiz. Coach (the chat panel on the right →) is here for any question — `Ask Coach` if anything is unclear.

steps.title: Install Fedora in VirtualBox (free RHEL-compatible)
steps.summary: Fedora is the upstream of RHEL — same package manager, same systemd, same Podman. And it's free.
steps.content_md:
## Why Fedora and not RHEL?

RHEL requires a paid subscription (or a free Developer Subscription with hoops to jump through). **Fedora** is the upstream that RHEL is built from — same `dnf`, same `systemd`, same Podman. For learning purposes it's identical *and free*.

## What you'll do

1. Install VirtualBox (free hypervisor) on your laptop
2. Download the Fedora Workstation ISO
3. Create a VM, boot the ISO, install Fedora

> **Tip from Coach**: 4 GB RAM and 2 CPU cores for the VM is enough. 30 GB disk.
instructions: Download VirtualBox 7.x for your OS: https://www.virtualbox.org/wiki/Downloads
Download Fedora Workstation ISO: https://fedoraproject.org/workstation/download
Open VirtualBox → New → Name: 'fedora-podman' → Type: Linux → Version: Fedora (64-bit)
Allocate 4096 MB RAM, 2 CPUs, 30 GB VDI disk
Settings → Storage → attach the Fedora ISO to the optical drive
Start the VM → click 'Install Fedora to Hard Drive' on the desktop
Follow installer: keyboard, language, target disk (auto), set root password + create user 'student'
Reboot, log in to your Fedora desktop
checkpoints: Fedora VM is created with 4 GB RAM and 30 GB disk
Fedora desktop has booted and you're logged in as 'student'

steps.title: Install Podman + verify
steps.summary: Fedora ships with Podman in the default repos. One command to install, one to verify.
steps.content_md:
## Install

Open the **Terminal** app on Fedora and run:

```bash
sudo dnf install -y podman
```

This pulls Podman and its dependencies (conmon, runc, slirp4netns, etc.).

## Verify

```bash
podman --version
podman info
```

The `info` command should show:
- `host.os: linux`
- `host.kernel: 6.x.x-...fc...`
- `store.graphRoot: /home/student/.local/share/containers/storage` (rootless!)

> **Why rootless matters**: Unlike Docker, Podman runs without a daemon and without root. Each user has their own container store. This is one of Red Hat's biggest selling points — and a common exam question.
instructions: Run `sudo dnf install -y podman` in the terminal
Run `podman --version` (expect 4.x or 5.x)
Run `podman info | grep -i graphRoot` to confirm rootless storage path
Run `podman run hello-world` — should pull and print 'Hello from Docker!'

steps.title: Module 1 Quiz
steps.summary: 5 questions on environment setup and the Podman vs Docker model.

questions.stem: Which is the most important reason Podman runs rootless by default?

options.text: Reduced attack surface — no privileged daemon listening on a socket

questions.explanation: Rootless Podman means no `dockerd`-style daemon running as root. If a container is compromised, it can't escalate to host root.

questions.stem: On Fedora, the rootless container store lives in:
questions.explanation: Each user gets their own container store under their home directory.

questions.stem: Why is Fedora a fine substitute for RHEL when learning EX188?

options.text: Fedora is the upstream of RHEL — same dnf, systemd, and Podman versions

questions.explanation: RHEL is downstream of Fedora; the container tooling stack is identical for the purposes of EX188 prep.

questions.stem: Which command installs Podman on Fedora?
questions.explanation: `dnf` is the package manager on modern Fedora and RHEL 8+.

questions.stem: What does `podman info` primarily show?

options.text: Host, store, registries and runtime configuration of your Podman install

modules.title: Module 2 — Podman Fundamentals
modules.summary: Run, stop, inspect, and remove containers. Understand the container lifecycle.

steps.title: Your first container — run, ps, logs
steps.summary: Run nginx in the background, see it in `podman ps`, follow its logs.
steps.content_md:
## Run a container

```bash
podman run -d --name web -p 8080:80 docker.io/library/nginx:latest
```

- `-d` detached (run in background)
- `--name web` give it a friendly name
- `-p 8080:80` map host port 8080 → container port 80
- The image is pulled from Docker Hub if not already local

## Verify

```bash
podman ps                # running containers
podman logs web          # see nginx access log
curl http://localhost:8080  # should return the nginx welcome page
```

## Stop and clean up

```bash
podman stop web
podman rm web
```
instructions: Run the nginx container detached on port 8080
Use `podman ps` to confirm it's running
`curl localhost:8080` and see the nginx HTML
Stop and remove the container
checkpoints: nginx is running in a detached container named 'web'
Container is stopped and removed cleanly

steps.title: Image vs Container — inspect both
steps.summary: An image is a read-only template; a container is a running instance with a writable layer.
steps.content_md:
## Mental model

- **Image** = baked, immutable, layered filesystem + metadata. Stored once.
- **Container** = running process(es) + a thin writable layer on top of the image.

## Useful commands

```bash
podman images                    # list local images
podman image inspect nginx       # JSON: layers, env, entrypoint, exposed ports
podman ps -a                      # containers including stopped
podman container inspect web     # JSON: state, mounts, networks, IP
podman diff web                   # what files the container changed vs its image
```

> **Coach tip**: When troubleshooting, `inspect` is your best friend. The `Config.Cmd` and `Config.Entrypoint` fields tell you what the container actually runs.
instructions: List your local images
Inspect the nginx image and find its `ExposedPorts`
Run a container, then inspect it to find its IP address
Make a change inside the container and use `podman diff` to see it

steps.title: Environment variables, port mapping, restart policy
steps.summary: Pass env vars, expose multiple ports, control auto-restart behaviour.
steps.content_md:
## Pass env vars

```bash
podman run -d --name db \
  -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=secret \
  -e POSTGRES_DB=app \
  -p 5432:5432 \
  --restart=unless-stopped \
  docker.io/library/postgres:16
```

## Restart policies (memorize for exam)

- `no` (default) — never restart
- `on-failure[:N]` — restart only if exit code != 0, optionally N times
- `always` — always restart
- `unless-stopped` — same as `always` except don't restart after manual stop

steps.title: Module 2 Quiz
steps.summary: 5 questions on lifecycle and basic flags.

questions.stem: Which flag combination runs a container detached and gives it the name 'web'?
An image is to a container as ___ is to ___.
Which command shows the IP address Podman assigned to a running container?
Which restart policy will NOT restart the container after the user manually `podman stop`s it?

options.text: All of the above will restart it

questions.stem: What does `podman diff <container>` show?

options.text: Files added/modified/deleted in the container's writable layer

modules.title: Module 3 — Building Images with Containerfile
modules.summary: Write a Containerfile (Podman's term for Dockerfile), build, tag, and inspect.

steps.title: Your first Containerfile
steps.summary: FROM, RUN, COPY, CMD — the four core instructions.
steps.content_md:
## What's a Containerfile?

It's the same as a Dockerfile — Podman just prefers the more vendor-neutral name. Both filenames work.

## The file's contents

The block below is the **contents of a file**, not commands you paste into the shell. Save it as `Containerfile` (no extension) inside a fresh directory.

```dockerfile
FROM registry.access.redhat.com/ubi9/python-39
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt
COPY . .
EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["gunicorn", "app:app", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
```

## Set it up

Run these in your terminal — this creates the directory, writes the Containerfile, and adds a tiny WSGI app + requirements.txt so the build has something to work with.

```bash
mkdir -p ~/myapp && cd ~/myapp

cat > Containerfile <<'CONTAINERFILE'
FROM registry.access.redhat.com/ubi9/python-39
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt
COPY . .
EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["gunicorn", "app:app", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
CONTAINERFILE

echo "gunicorn" > requirements.txt

cat > app.py <<'APPPY'
def app(environ, start_response):
    start_response("200 OK", [("Content-Type", "text/plain")])
    return [b"hello from podman\n"]
APPPY
```

## Build and run

```bash
podman build -t myapp:1.0 .
podman images | grep myapp
podman run -d -p 8000:8000 --name app myapp:1.0
curl http://localhost:8000   # → hello from podman
```
instructions: Create the directory `~/myapp` and `cd` into it
Save the Containerfile contents above into a file literally named `Containerfile` (use the `cat > Containerfile <<EOF` block in the setup section)
Add `requirements.txt` (just `gunicorn`) and a tiny `app.py` so the build has something to copy
Build with `podman build -t myapp:1.0 .`
Run it and `curl http://localhost:8000` to verify the response

steps.title: Multi-stage builds + image size
steps.summary: Use a builder stage to compile, then copy artefacts into a slim runtime image.
steps.content_md:
## Why multi-stage?

A Go binary needs a 700 MB build environment to compile. The runtime needs nothing but the binary. Multi-stage builds keep the final image tiny.

```dockerfile
FROM golang:1.22 AS build
WORKDIR /src
COPY . .
RUN go build -o /out/app ./cmd/app

FROM registry.access.redhat.com/ubi9-minimal
COPY --from=build /out/app /usr/local/bin/app
CMD ["app"]
```

Before: ~900 MB. After: ~120 MB.

steps.title: Tagging, pushing, and pulling from a registry
steps.summary: Push an image to quay.io (free) using your Red Hat account.
steps.content_md:
## Tag and push

```bash
podman tag myapp:1.0 quay.io/myuser/myapp:1.0
podman login quay.io      # interactive — enter your quay.io credentials
podman push quay.io/myuser/myapp:1.0
```

## Pull from anywhere

```bash
podman pull quay.io/myuser/myapp:1.0
```

> **Exam tip**: The exam may use a private registry. Practice `podman login <registry>` and verify with `cat $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/containers/auth.json`.

steps.title: Module 3 Quiz
steps.summary: 5 questions on building, tagging, and pushing images.

questions.stem: What's the relationship between a Containerfile and a Dockerfile?

options.text: Identical syntax — Containerfile is just a vendor-neutral name
Containerfile is for OCI; Dockerfile is for legacy Docker
Containerfile only works with Podman; Dockerfile only with Docker

questions.stem: In a multi-stage build, the `COPY --from=build` syntax does what?

options.text: Copies from a previous build stage's filesystem

questions.stem: Where does Podman store registry login credentials by default (rootless)?
Which command builds an image tagged `webapp:dev` from the current directory?
Which Red Hat registry hosts officially-supported UBI base images?

modules.title: Module 4 — Pods (Multi-Container Apps)
modules.summary: Pods are Podman's killer feature: multiple containers sharing a network namespace, like Kubernetes.

steps.title: Why pods? The web + db pattern
steps.summary: A pod = N containers + 1 shared network namespace.
steps.content_md:
## The killer feature

A **pod** lets multiple containers share `localhost`. Your web container can talk to the database on `localhost:5432` even though they're separate containers.

This is the same model Kubernetes uses, and it's where 'Podman' got its name.

## When to use a pod

- A web app that needs an attached cache (Redis sidecar)
- An app + log shipper sidecar
- Anything you'd put in a single Kubernetes pod

steps.title: Create a pod with web + postgres
steps.summary: One pod, two containers, shared localhost.
steps.content_md:
## Steps

```bash
# 1. Create the pod (publishes port 8080 to host)
podman pod create --name webapp -p 8080:80

# 2. Add the postgres container
podman run -d --pod webapp \
  -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=secret \
  -e POSTGRES_DB=app \
  --name db postgres:16

# 3. Add the web container — talks to postgres on localhost!
podman run -d --pod webapp \
  -e DATABASE_URL=postgres://postgres:secret@localhost:5432/app \
  --name web nginx

# 4. Inspect
podman pod ps           # see your pod
podman pod inspect webapp
podman ps --pod         # containers grouped by pod
```
instructions: Create a pod called 'webapp' that publishes port 8080
Add a postgres container into that pod
Add an nginx (or your app) container into that pod
Verify both are running with `podman ps --pod`

steps.title: Generate Kubernetes YAML from a pod
steps.summary: podman generate kube — your pod becomes a real K8s manifest.
steps.content_md:
## Why this matters

Podman pods speak the same shape as Kubernetes pods. You can build and test locally, then export a manifest for your team's K8s cluster.

```bash
podman generate kube webapp > webapp.yaml
cat webapp.yaml          # valid Kubernetes Pod YAML
```

This is a tested exam topic — know the command.

steps.title: Module 4 Quiz
steps.summary: 5 questions on pods.

questions.stem: Containers in the same pod share which Linux namespaces?

options.text: All of them — same as a single container
Only the network and IPC namespaces (others are separate)

questions.explanation: Network + IPC are shared so containers can talk on localhost. Mount, PID, UTS namespaces stay isolated.

questions.stem: Which command exports a Podman pod as a Kubernetes manifest?
If your web container talks to its database via `localhost:5432`, that database must be:

options.text: Running in another container in the SAME pod
Running in another container in any pod

questions.stem: Port mappings (`-p`) on a pod's containers are:

options.text: Set on the POD itself with `podman pod create -p`

questions.stem: What's the special 'infra' container Podman creates inside each pod for?

options.text: Holds the shared network namespace alive even if other containers die

modules.title: Module 5 — Storage, Networking, Quadlets
modules.summary: Persistent volumes, custom networks, and systemd integration with Quadlet.

steps.title: Volumes: persistent storage
steps.summary: Named volumes vs bind mounts vs tmpfs.
steps.content_md:
## Three mount types

```bash
# 1. Named volume — Podman manages the location
podman volume create pgdata
podman run -d --name db -v pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data postgres:16

# 2. Bind mount — host path
podman run -d --name web -v /home/student/site:/usr/share/nginx/html:Z nginx

# 3. tmpfs — RAM, gone on stop
podman run -d --tmpfs /tmp/cache nginx
```

> **The `:Z` flag is critical on Fedora/RHEL** — it sets the SELinux label so the container can access the bind mount. Without it you'll get permission denied. This is a top-3 exam gotcha.
instructions: Create a named volume called 'pgdata'
Run postgres with the volume mounted
Stop and remove the container
Recreate it — your data is still there
Try a bind mount with and without `:Z` to see SELinux behaviour

steps.title: Custom networks
steps.summary: Create a network, connect containers, resolve by name.
steps.content_md:
```bash
podman network create app-net
podman run -d --network app-net --name db -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=x postgres
podman run -d --network app-net --name web nginx
# 'web' can reach 'db' as hostname `db` (DNS provided by aardvark-dns)
```

steps.title: Quadlet: run containers as systemd services
steps.summary: The modern (RHEL 9+) way to make containers auto-start and be managed by systemd.
steps.content_md:
## What's a Quadlet?

A `.container` file dropped under `~/.config/containers/systemd/` (rootless) or `/etc/containers/systemd/` (rootful). Systemd auto-generates a real `.service` unit from it.

## Example

`~/.config/containers/systemd/web.container`

```ini
[Unit]
Description=My web app
After=network-online.target

[Container]
Image=quay.io/myuser/myapp:1.0
PublishPort=8080:80
Volume=app-data:/data:Z

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target
```

## Activate

```bash
systemctl --user daemon-reload
systemctl --user start web.service
systemctl --user enable web.service       # auto-start on login
loginctl enable-linger student            # keep services running when user logs out
```

> **Quadlet is the new exam favourite** — it's the official replacement for `podman generate systemd` (deprecated in 5.0+). Know the file format.

steps.title: Module 5 Quiz
steps.summary: 5 questions on storage, networks, Quadlets.

questions.stem: On Fedora/RHEL, omitting the `:Z` flag on a bind mount typically causes:

options.text: The mount to fail at start
Permission denied errors inside the container due to SELinux
The host directory to be deleted

questions.stem: Containers on the same custom Podman network can resolve each other by:
Where do rootless Quadlet `.container` files live?
What does `loginctl enable-linger <user>` do?

options.text: Keeps user systemd services running after they log out
Adds the user to the docker group

questions.stem: Which is the OFFICIALLY RECOMMENDED Red Hat way to run Podman containers as systemd services on RHEL 9+?

modules.title: Module 6 — Final Prep
modules.summary: Mock scenarios, exam-day strategy, and a 20-question practice test.

steps.title: Top 10 exam-day commands to memorize
steps.summary: The commands that come up over and over in EX188 tasks.
steps.content_md:
## Memorize these patterns

```bash
# Run with all the bells
podman run -d --name X -p 8080:80 -v vol:/data:Z -e KEY=val --restart=always image:tag

# Build & push
podman build -t quay.io/me/app:1 . && podman push quay.io/me/app:1

# Pod with an app + sidecar
podman pod create --name p -p 8080:80
podman run -d --pod p --name app image

# Quadlet skeleton — under ~/.config/containers/systemd/X.container
# [Container]
# Image=...
# PublishPort=...
# Volume=...:Z

# Generate K8s YAML
podman generate kube p > p.yaml

# Inspect & debug
podman logs -f X
podman exec -it X bash
podman container inspect X
```

steps.title: Mock scenario: 'Run a web app + cache + db as a unit'
steps.summary: A typical exam task. Coach can grade your solution if you describe it.
steps.content_md:
## Task

> Create a pod called `shop` exposing port `8080:80`. Inside the pod, run:
> - A `valkey:latest` container named `cache` with `requirepass=hunter2`
> - A `postgres:16` container named `db` with password `hunter2` and database `shop`
> - An `nginx:latest` container named `web` (placeholder for the real app)
> Make all three persist across reboots using Quadlet.

Write the commands you'd run, then **`Ask Coach`** to grade them.

steps.title: Final 20-question practice test
steps.summary: Mixed difficulty, all modules. 70% to pass.

questions.stem: Which command shows ALL containers (running + stopped)?
Rootless Podman networks use which DNS resolver by default on modern systems?
The `--rm` flag on `podman run` does what?

options.text: Removes the container after it exits
Removes any existing container with the same name

questions.stem: Which OCI runtime does Podman use by default?
An image tag like `nginx:1.25-alpine` is roughly:

options.text: A mutable label that can point to different image digests over time
Always pulled fresh from the registry

questions.stem: How do you inject a multi-line config file into a container without baking it in?

options.text: Bind mount the file from the host with -v

questions.stem: Best command to stream live logs from a container?
podman exec -it <c> bash gives you:

options.text: A new bash process inside the running container

questions.stem: A Containerfile USER instruction sets:

options.text: The OS user that container processes run as
The user that mounted the volumes

questions.stem: Default healthcheck retries before marking unhealthy?
What's wrong with `podman run -p 80:8080 nginx`?

options.text: Privileged port 80 needs root or sysctl tweak in rootless mode

questions.stem: Which file controls the registries Podman searches when you `podman pull nginx` (no registry prefix)?
`podman cp` copies between:

options.text: Host and container (or container and container)

questions.stem: What does `podman system prune` do?

options.text: Removes stopped containers, dangling images, and unused networks

questions.stem: Which command shows real-time CPU/memory usage of containers?
If a container's process is PID 1 inside the container, on the host it appears as:

options.text: A different, normal PID — namespaces remap
An init process owned by root

questions.stem: podman save outputs:

options.text: A tar archive of the image

questions.stem: podman play kube does what?

options.text: Runs a Kubernetes Pod YAML against a real K8s cluster
Re-creates a Pod/Deployment locally from K8s YAML

questions.stem: Which is true about rootless container UIDs?

options.text: All processes are root inside AND outside
Root inside is mapped to your unprivileged UID outside via subuid

questions.stem: Best practice for SECRETS in containers?

options.text: Use podman secret create + --secret on run, or mount from a controlled file

title: AWS SAA-C03 Foundations Path
description: A 6-module guided tour through the SAA-C03 essentials. Pairs with the existing 8,800-question SparkUpCloud bank.

modules.title: Module 1 — Pillars of the Well-Architected Framework
modules.summary: The 6 pillars and how AWS expects you to think.

steps.title: Why architects, not just operators
steps.summary: SAA tests trade-offs, not commands.
steps.content_md:
## The mindset shift

SAA is **not** a memorization test. Every question gives you a scenario where multiple AWS services would technically work — your job is to pick the one with the right trade-off.

The 6 pillars frame those trade-offs:

1. **Operational Excellence** — can you operate this at scale?
2. **Security** — least privilege, encryption everywhere
3. **Reliability** — survives AZ failure, recovers from region failure
4. **Performance Efficiency** — right-sized, right-service
5. **Cost Optimization** — you're not paying for unused capacity
6. **Sustainability** — efficient use of resources

When two answers seem right, ask: which pillar is the question really testing?

steps.title: Module 1 Quiz
steps.summary: Pillar identification.

questions.stem: A question asks how to design 'minimal downtime' on AZ failure. Which pillar?
A question asks how to reduce monthly spend on dev environments. Which pillar?
